channel.title: NYT > Climate and Environment
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Taking Out the Trash: These Old Ladies Are Cleaning Up Cape Cod
item.description: The Old Ladies Against Underwater Garbage estimate they’ve removed 6,000 pounds of refuse from ponds on Cape Cod. “There’s no better feeling,” one said.
item.dc:creator: Cara Buckley and Billy Hickey
item.media:credit: Billy Hickey for The New York Times

item.title: How Companies Have Abandoned Their Climate Goals and Let Themselves Off the Hook
item.description: Big business made big promises about saving the planet. Following through hasn’t been easy.
item.dc:creator: David Gelles

item.title: The Latest Texas Floods Tested Warning Systems. This Time, They Passed.
item.description: A lack of sirens and flood forecasting systems made the state’s Hill Country vulnerable to deadly floods last summer. That has changed.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance and Edgar Sandoval
item.media:credit: Brandon Bell/Getty Images
item.media:description: Flood debris blocked a road in Kerrville, Texas, on Thursday.

item.title: Trump Administration’s Cuts Targeted Wildfire Research
item.description: Labs at the Environmental Protection Agency and the Forest Service have analyzed the effects of wildfires. The administration wants to shutter them.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Lee Zeldin, the E.P.A. administrator, with President Trump at the White House in February.

item.title: The Science of Climate Change Explained: Facts, Evidence and Proof
item.description: Definitive answers to the big questions.
item.dc:creator: Julia Rosen
item.category: United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change

item.title: On Fire. Again.
item.description: There were orange skies in 2023, 2025 and now again in 2026. Over the last few years, one thing has become glaringly clear: The East Coast is totally unequipped for the next climate disaster, wildfires. The Opinion writer David Wallace-Wells explains that with every climate disaster, we’re not just adapting to a “new normal,” but bracing ourselves for what’s next.
item.dc:creator: David Wallace-Wells

item.title: Climate Change Contributes to Rising Share of Wildfire Smoke Deaths
item.description: Wildfire smoke is linked to tens of thousands of deaths a year, a number that is rising with climate change.
item.dc:creator: Raymond Zhong
item.media:credit: Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Smoky haze engulfing Chicago on Thursday.

item.title: E.U. Proposes Changes to Emissions Trading System
item.description: The European Union is facing a green backlash as emissions cuts become harder and require bigger overhauls to industry and livelihoods
item.dc:creator: Chico Harlan
item.category: Carbon Caps and Emissions Trading Programs
item.media:credit: Nadja Wohlleben/Reuters
item.media:description: Europe, which often holds itself up as a climate leader, has been facing political infighting over some of its policies.

item.title: As Blazes Spread Near Paris, Extreme Heat Is Changing How France Fights Fires
item.description: Until recently, France’s forest fires were mostly in the south. This week, fires are raging much farther north. France is having to rethink its firefighting strategy.
item.dc:creator: Catherine Porter and Dmitry Kostyukov
item.media:credit: Dmitry Kostyukov for The New York Times

item.title: Canada’s Wildfires Are Burning Through the Night
item.description: As overnight temperatures climb across Canada, fires that once spread more slowly after dark are burning around the clock, straining fire crews.
item.dc:creator: Vjosa Isai and Amy Graff
item.media:credit: Ian Willms for The New York Times
item.media:description: The observation deck of the CN Tower on Wednesday evening as wildfire smoke enveloped downtown Toronto.

item.title: Climate Change Contributes to a Smokier World
item.description: Higher temperatures and drought conditions are contributing to more intense wildfires and extending the summer fire season in North America.
item.dc:creator: Quinn Glabicki
item.media:credit: Nick Hagen for The New York Times
item.media:description: A stretch of highway in Southeast Michigan under thick smoke on Thursday.

item.title: Burning Houses to Save Houses
item.description: We visited researchers seeking ways to make homes safer. They set a lot of fires.
item.dc:creator: Mira Rojanasakul
item.media:credit: Will Crooks for The New York Times
item.media:description: A wildfire test at the Insurance Institute for Business & Home Safety lab in Richburg, S.C.

item.title: Mamdani Urges New Yorkers to Stay Indoors Because of Wildfire Smoke
item.description: “Every New Yorker should take precautions,” Mayor Zohran Mamdani said. Gov. Kathy Hochul also issued a statewide air quality advisory.
item.dc:creator: Sally Goldenberg
item.media:credit: Angelina Katsanis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Smoke filled the sky over New York City on Thursday morning.

item.title: National Academies Report Backs Climate Change Attribution Science
item.description: Attribution science is advancing quickly, researchers said. That could support lawsuits seeking damages for severe events worsened by global warming.
item.dc:creator: Raymond Zhong
item.category: National Academies of Sciences, Engineering and Medicine
item.media:credit: Stephane Mahe/Reuters
item.media:description: A firefighting helicopter at a wildfire burning in the Forest of Fontainebleau near Paris on Monday.

item.title: What Can We Learn From Burning Down This House?
item.description: Wildfires are intensifying. Our climate reporter Mira Rojanasakul goes inside an unusual lab working to protect homes from the flames.
item.dc:creator: Mira Rojanasakul, Christina Shaman, June Kim, Dillon Deaton, Will Crooks and Isaac Cropsey

item.title: Door-to-Door Search for Grapevines Sold at Costco Begins in California’s Santa Clara County
item.description: Officials in Santa Clara County began knocking on doors on Monday to collect potentially infested plants sold at some Costco stores.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra E. Petri
item.media:credit: Patrick T. Fallon/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Santa Clara County also warned that desert willows sold at certain Costco locations across 15 Northern California counties between late June 24 and July 3 might also be affected.

item.title: Canada Wildfire Air Pollution and Heat Wave Combo Pose Heightened Health Risks
item.description: Days that are both extremely hot and polluted come with higher risks of respiratory ailments and other health hazards.
item.dc:creator: Claire Brown and Christina Kelso
item.media:credit: Laura Proctor/The Canadian Press, via Associated Press
item.media:description: A hazy street in Toronto as wildfire smoke blanketed the city on Wednesday.

item.title: Data Centers to Add Billions in Power Costs in 13 States
item.description: A power auction conducted by a giant grid operator is expected to add $6.3 billion in additional charges to consumers and businesses because of electricity needs of data centers.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Penn
item.media:credit: Leah Millis/Reuters
item.media:description: Northern Virginia is home to several data centers, including the Digital Realty Data Center in Ashburn.

item.title: An Extraordinary White House Meeting
item.description: A new book sheds light on President Trump’s affinity for fossil fuels.
item.dc:creator: David Gelles
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: An oil refinery in Punto Fijo, Venezuela. In a meeting with the president, the Chevron chief executive pushed for an extension of the company’s license to operate in the country.

item.title: Roger Worthington Is Suing Over a Heat Wave
item.description: Roger Worthington is helping to lead a $50 billion lawsuit against oil producers. This week he’ll face powerful critics in Congress.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.media:credit: Michael Hanson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Roger Worthington in a garden adjacent to his brewery in Bend, Ore.

item.title: Dark Sky Defenders Raise Alarm Along the Border of West Texas
item.description: Residents fear that the Trump administration’s plans to build a border wall will threaten the region’s spectacular starry skies.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller and Emily Elconin
item.media:credit: Emily Elconin for The New York Times

item.title: Trump Sharply Cuts the Size of Two National Monuments in Utah
item.description: Native American tribes and environmental groups are expected to challenge the move to shrink the Bears Ears and Grand Staircase-Escalante National Monuments.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow
item.media:credit: Wolfgang Kaehler/LightRocket, via Getty Images
item.media:description: A hiker in the Grand Staircase-Escalante National Monument in Utah.

item.title: Another Heat Wave Brings Unbearable Temperatures to Parts of the U.S.
item.description: Salt Lake City reached its highest temperature ever recorded this weekend.
item.dc:creator: Nazaneen Ghaffar
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: U.S. Electric Vehicle Sales are Down but E.V. Chargers are Booming
item.description: Electric vehicle chargers are proliferating in Southern states as fast food restaurants, stores and other businesses try to lure customers.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing
item.media:credit: Gavin McIntyre for The New York Times
item.media:description: A Bojangles in Savannah, Ga., recently added E.V. charging stations outside the restaurant.

item.title: Heat Waves in Europe Linked to Thousands of Excess Deaths
item.description: While the full toll will take months to calculate, death statistics already look substantial compared with historical averages.
item.dc:creator: Amelia Nierenberg
item.media:credit: Tom Nicholson/Reuters

item.title: Housing or Flood Protection? In Brooklyn, Housing Is Winning.
item.description: A towering apartment development has been approved for the waterfront, even though some residents would have preferred parkland.
item.dc:creator: Hilary Howard
item.media:credit: Lexi Parra/The New York Times
item.media:description: This portion of the Brooklyn waterfront is a former oil and gas hub. Much of it still needs to be cleaned up.

item.title: Residents of Spanish Towns Caught in Fierce Blazes Recount Harrowing Escapes
item.description: Many say communication was chaotic and even nonexistent as flames tore through the area, taking 12 lives in one of the deadliest wildfires on record in Spain.
item.dc:creator: Jason Horowitz, Carlos Barragán and Lynsey Chutel
item.media:credit: Pablo Blazquez Dominguez/Getty Images

item.title: In the Hamptons, Even the Chicken Tenders Are Living Fancy
item.description: It’s summer on the South Fork. Care for some nuggets and caviar?
item.dc:creator: Dionne Searcey
item.media:credit: Emma Rose Milligan for The New York Times
item.media:description: Partygoers at the Surf Lodge in Montauk enjoy a chicken tender tower.

item.title: F.C.C. Approves Test of Space Mirror to Light Night Sky Despite Outcry
item.description: A start-up company has permission to try its plan to bounce solar rays onto the dark side of Earth, turning night to day for a three-mile-wide patch.
item.dc:creator: Hiroko Tabuchi
item.media:credit: Reflect Orbital
item.media:description: An artist’s concept of space mirrors in orbit.

item.title: Flooding in Missouri Prompts State of Emergency and at Least 100 Rescues
item.description: Gov. Mike Kehoe declared a state of emergency after up to a foot of rain fell in the southeastern part of the state. More storms are in the forecast.
item.dc:creator: John S.W. MacDonald and Amy Graff

item.title: Trump Cuts Habitat Protections for Endangered Species
item.description: The rule change ends a safeguard that had been in place for 50 years and could hasten the demise of imperiled animals.
item.dc:creator: Catrin Einhorn and Maxine Joselow
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
item.media:description: Piping plovers need undisturbed beaches to nest and raise their young.

item.title: Trump Administration Taps Climate Science Critic to Oversee National Climate Assessment
item.description: Matthew M. Wielicki has criticized the scientific consensus on global warming as alarmist. Now he’ll oversee a major federal report on the topic.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow and Brad Plumer
item.media:credit: Go Nakamura for The New York Times
item.media:description: A scorched road in Hawaii in 2023 after devastating wildfires. The last climate assessment, published that year, found that human-caused global warming was supercharging wildfires in the West, droughts in the Great Plains and heat waves across the country.

item.title: U.S. Department of Energy Underestimated Potential Los Alamos Plutonium Leak Danger, Study Finds
item.description: An accident at the lab that produces America’s nuclear bomb cores could lead to more fatalities than previously estimated by the federal government, according to new research.
item.dc:creator: Alicia Inez Guzmán
item.media:credit: Nina Riggio for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Los Alamos National Laboratory in New Mexico is home to the country’s only active plutonium-handling facility.

item.title: She Studied Ways to Make People Smarter. Then Her Grant Was Cut.
item.description: Jessica Cantlon had a grant with the U.S. Navy to explore whether people could improve spatial problem-solving skills through training. Then the program was cut.
item.dc:creator: Sachi Kitajima Mulkey
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Jake Reinhart for The New York Times

item.title: Ticks Are on the Move. Here Are the Risks in Your Region.
item.description: Changing deer populations, rising temperatures and other factors are driving ticks and their diseases into new areas.
item.dc:creator: Brianna Abbott
item.media:credit: Bgwalker/Getty Images

item.title: He Wanted to Track Microplastics in the Sea. The E.P.A. Fired Him.
item.description: The official urged requiring a fish farm to monitor for the tiny plastic particles. The E.P.A. said he had taken steps to embarrass the agency.
item.dc:creator: Hiroko Tabuchi

item.title: Fighting Fires With Figures, These Experts Are Trying to Stop Europe From Burning
item.description: A team of engineers, foresters and scientists is helping the continent prepare for wildfires from a giant science park in Italy. Their arsenal includes satellites, weather models and expert analysis.
item.dc:creator: Jeanna Smialek
item.media:credit: Aris Messinis/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Firefighters in Patras, Greece, in August. National authorities fight fires within their borders, but the European Union helps coordinate responses and sends additional resources to places in need.

item.title: New York Sues Companies Over ‘Forever Chemicals’
item.description: The lawsuit claims that the companies, including 3M and DuPont, contaminated the environment and engaged in fraud by withholding information from the public about the harms of the chemicals, known as PFAS.
item.dc:creator: Christina Caron and Hilary Howard
item.media:credit: Vincent Alban/The New York Times
item.media:description: Letitia James, the New York attorney general, said that chemical companies operating in the state “knowingly sold toxic products.”

item.title: Advanced Recycling Hits a Rough Patch
item.description: In the past weeks, two sites in the United States have halted operations.
item.dc:creator: Hiroko Tabuchi
item.media:credit: Maddie McGarvey for The New York Times
item.media:description: Unlike traditional mechanical recycling, advanced recycling can theoretically process mixed or contaminated plastics that otherwise would end up in landfills.

item.title: Trump’s Plans for Nuclear Power Lurch Ahead
item.description: Recent technological advances have brought the president’s goals a bit closer to reality.
item.dc:creator: Claire Brown
item.media:credit: Josh Edelson/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Energy Secretary Chris Wright, center, visited the National Reactor Innovation Center near Idaho Falls, Idaho, last month.

item.title: E.P.A. Moves to Loosen Limits on Pollution From Trucks
item.description: The Trump administration wants to roll back some Biden-era restrictions on smog-causing emissions from heavy-duty vehicles.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow
item.media:credit: Justin Sullivan/Getty Images
item.media:description: Trucks at the Port of Oakland. The new proposal would rescind a 2022 rule designed to significantly reduce emissions of nitrogen oxides, which cause smog.

item.title: I.U.C.N. Update Says Deep-Sea Mining Threatens Mollusks Around Hydrothermal Vents
item.description: The snails and other mollusks around hydrothermal vents have evolved to thrive in extreme conditions, but mineral extraction could drive more than half to extinction.
item.dc:creator: Catrin Einhorn
item.category: International Union for Conservation of Nature

item.title: Western Europe Had Its Hottest June on Record
item.description: An unusually early heat wave smashed records across France, Britain and Spain. Temperatures in some areas are starting to soar again.
item.dc:creator: Raymond Zhong
item.media:credit: Kin Cheung/Associated Press
item.media:description: Riding a hot train in London on June 24. Average temperatures across Western Europe last month were 3.05 degrees Celsius, or 5.5 degrees Fahrenheit, above the mean from recent decades.

item.title: Spread of Seafloor Directly Observed for the First Time
item.description: The spread of the ocean floor, as tectonic plates spread apart, is known but hard to observe. Scientists have now documented the process in action.
item.dc:creator: K. R. Callaway
item.media:credit: Benoit Stichelbaut/Hemis, via Alamy
item.media:description: Île Saint-Paul, a tiny island in the Indian Ocean, some 120 miles southwest of the deep sea site where the Antarctica and Australia tectonic plates diverged during an earthquake in 2024.

item.title: Oil Market Calm Shattered by Fresh Hostilities Between US and Iran
item.description: Oil prices are likely to remain volatile as long as the United States and Iran are unable to resolve the status of the Strait of Hormuz.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott and Lisa Friedman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: Hotter Seas in Britain and Europe Threaten Marine Life
item.description: Higher sea temperatures have followed scorching weather across Europe, prompting Britain’s weather service to declare a “severe” marine heat wave.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Wolfe
item.media:credit: Carlos Jasso/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: People diving into the English Channel in Brighton, on the south coast of England, during a heat wave last month.

item.title: Global Warming Could Ravage the Amazon’s Useful Plant Species, Study Finds
item.description: Hotter temperatures and harsher droughts could cause Indigenous societies to lose many of the species they have used for medicine, rituals and more, scientists found.
item.dc:creator: Raymond Zhong and Flávia Milhorance
item.media:credit: Victor Moriyama for The New York Times
item.media:description: The study warned that major losses of plant species could occur in the coming decades even if nations limit global warming to moderate levels.

item.title: New Rules Require Election Changes for States to Get Terrorism Grants
item.description: Federal officials said they would withhold some money unless states pursue paper ballot systems, verify citizenship and conduct costly audits.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance, Nick Corasaniti and Hilary Howard
item.media:credit: Emily Elconin for The New York Times
item.media:description: Courts have already blocked similar efforts by the administration to withhold funding, saying the Constitution does not grant the executive branch power over elections.

item.title: Trump’s Top Air Pollution Regulator Will Resign
item.description: Aaron Szabo will step down as the head of the Environmental Protection Agency’s air office this month, he told employees, after overseeing rollbacks of a range of pollution limits.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow and Lisa Friedman
item.media:credit: Annabelle Gordon/Sipa USA, via Alamy Live News
item.media:description: Aaron Szabo, right, during a confirmation hearing last year to lead the Environmental Protection Agency’s Office of Air and Radiation.

item.title: Nation’s 250th Celebration Fireworks Sent D.C. Air Pollution Spiking
item.description: The president promised a record display of some 850,000 pyrotechnics for the country’s 250th birthday. The Park Service warned it could get dirty.
item.dc:creator: Quinn Glabicki
item.media:credit: Alex Kent/The New York Times
item.media:description: The pollution began dissipating by morning, though it lingered longer in neighborhoods to the north of the city.

item.title: As Ice Melts in the Arctic, Some Deep-Sea Creatures Are Thriving
item.description: A new study suggests that deep-sea life reaps the benefits of icebergs’ castoffs — a rare silver lining as a warming planet destabilizes glacial ice.
item.dc:creator: K. R. Callaway

item.title: I Wanted an Ecologically Responsible Garden. It Was Harder Than I Thought.
item.description: The native plant movement gets a lot right, but there’s so much more to consider.
item.dc:creator: Ferris Jabr
item.media:credit: Balarama Heller for The New York Times
item.media:description: A field of Phacelia blooming on the property of Douglas Tallamy.

item.title: Inside the United States’ Billion-Dollar Blind Spot: Hail
item.description: Scientists have never quite understood how hail works. Meet the storm chasers trying to change that.
item.dc:creator: Judson Jones
item.media:credit: Vincent Alban/The New York Times

item.title: Death Toll From Oppressive Heat in New Jersey May Be as High as 29
item.description: In New York City, emergency rooms last week logged the highest number of patients treated for heat-related illnesses in years.
item.dc:creator: Tracey Tully and Joseph Goldstein
item.media:credit: Hiroko Masuike/The New York Times
item.media:description: Soccer fans sought a cool spot last week at a World Cup match at MetLife Stadium in New Jersey.
